author: @nealgallagher4684
text:
Build a enclosure it doesn't need to be Fred dibnah strength I've seen cardboard boxes used
it's much easier to live with in-house, put a fire extinguisher ballon in the enclosure and a cheap webcam. As for the fumes a 4" duct fan and a couple of metres of ducting cut a
hole out of the enclosure and vent outside, if you can't vent outside or bad weather get a 5
gallon home brew bucket put a 4" hole in the middle of the lid put lot's of smaller holes 
in the lid half fill the bucket with water and add 3 or 4 drops of washing machine cleaner 
to the water, its anti bacterial and smells nice comes in different flavours put the ducting to the bottom of the bucket then just change the water once a  month I use the same bucket technique for airbrushing. hope this is helpful

author: @matteonicoli
text: does it print QR-codes on plastic?   And does it print QR-codes on slightly curved surfaces like power tools?

replies.author: @algolaserofficial
replies.text: It can print QR codes on plastic except transparent plastic. And it can also print on curved surfaces, but need one AlgoLaser Y-axis Rotary Chuck (ARC).

author: @VeniceInventors
text: A couple of advantages of the open-frame design are having the ability to engrave or cut very large areas the size of a table or door, and being able to hang it on the wall to save space when not in use.

text:
He Robert,
Hope you are doing well.
This is not a comment on this video, but I got a news article on a new windmill.
I was impressed by the innovation of a weathervane that blocks the blades going against the wind.
I thought you might find it interesting (there's also something about gears)
They applied for a patent, in production 2025. 
Celltechnologies. The blade, website is a point NL.
You probably could read it with translate.
Have a nice evening and stay well 👍🖐️

author: @bsc4344
text:
ok.

turn laser head sideways  and enable it to spin on axis like an old vinyl LP in orientation... . stack a “glue” or malleable extrusion nozzle assembly in a shared rackrail placed  fashion above laser assembly.  A “liquid plastic glue gun” nozzle output, that gets its hardening not from heat but superior SPECIFIC WAVELENGTH of beam that follows the extruded nozzle up the framework guides, rotating from centreline and sharing the same scaffold outer frame, independent from the extruded. 

Would have to be super FAST to outpace the follow up curing laser, so the extrusion material could be pretty watery, actually...

wouldn’t that be faster stronger and better than the micro bead 3D printing manner that requires a chamber full of material that very slowly raises from the current process that takes forever?

The process would be akin to the current marketed “dentist developed adherence liquid” that cures with accompanied UV handheld mini light....    watery agent, stays fluid but hardened in mere seconds only after beaming UV (specific wavelength ) at it....

ok, shoot idea down now keeners...

author: @CrudelyMade
text:
lightburn is 1000% better than lasergrbl.  worth the price.  I've used both, and now I use lightburn for 1/2 of my designing, because it's good at that too.  and it works with my fiber laser, diode laser, and CO2 lasers...   I've found 0 fault with it, love its features, and it'd be hard to go back to the very limited (relatively) lasergrbl.  
I started with lasergrbl but eded up in lightburn.  and.. it's actually easier than lasergrbl for so many things.   I cann't say enough good things about it. 
it's like comparing a pedal car to a real car.  while importing svg files and running them through is easy enough..  the editing features are also pretty rich and that's where most of the learning curve would be if you're not familiar with vector graphics..  but if you are, it'll be a rather shallow curve and there are tons of youtube videos covering how to do a thing. 
also, it's been around long enough that AI can help with how to do things in that software as well.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
I have seen people talking about the costs to get tools and everyone who complained about the cost of say a laser cutter, I immediately thought about my issue of not being able to afford such a thing, so I just build one using cheaper diode based lasers, and avoiding trying to cut anything clear, which solved my problem. Not sure why a clear substance is required that also would require the technical cutting skill of a laser, so... I guess if someone has an instance, lets hear it. (engraving is not hard, I just use sharpie or some other colorant that will absorb and can be used to coat the material, one good one is these high temp sharpies meant for use on really hot items and not degrade, which means it allows the laser to raise its temp much higher bitting into a clear substance under it better to get a good engraving shape. ) 

My big thing is the metal cutting that I found a way that uses a combination of electrolysis and how laser printers work to clear an area of a surface by hitting it with the laser temporarily making it very conductive to extract the value of electrons dumped on it to attract positively charge (or repell negatively charge) toner powder, the power level is so incredibly low to alter this substance's conductivity from an insulator to a conductor so..
